The encounter we're going to talk about tonight, Mark, happened on your grandparents' property.

I come from the Ozark Mountains, the foothills of the Ozark Mountains.
You have to go a little bit north to get into the full Ozarks, but it's full of hills.
It's full of woods.
I would say less than 100 acres of that property was actual pasture land, and that was just the stuff around the house.
The rest of the woods, the cattle and stuff would stay there.
One of the things about it, when I was very, very young, my grandfather had taken me out.
I was about 10 when he passed away.
So this would have been the late 70s.
He took us out, he took me out,
he took me into this place And to this day I tried to find it and I can find it but I know it there because I seen it So he took me out and showed me what is basically well it not basic to it
It's an Indian burial ground. I would say there was anywhere from 20 to 30 burial grounds. And
I'm talking old stuff from probably the late, maybe mid 1800s, because this was all Indian
territory back in those days. And, you know, we've obviously moved this way and occupied this part of
the country. So we had that. And all along the river that I was talking about runs through my
grandparents' land. It's a lot of bottom land. It's a lot of open fields. And these were big
Indian settlements back in the day. You can walk down on that river. You can just darn near

related whether it be an arrowhead or part of an arrowhead or even maybe a spear you know the tip
of the spear or something the arrowheads and the spear tips are obviously much different
so the place has a lot of history a lot of history that we don't know about a lot of history that

I imagine it's just lost to time. Who knows? We also had the state had come in and leased several acres from my grandparents to quarry stone and rocks, basically just for building highways and whatnot.
I would say back in the 80s and early 90s, if you drove on a road in northwest Arkansas, that gravel probably came from my grandparents quarry.
And so that, and with it being a farm, there's not a whole lot special about it.
Just the Indian history, I think, and the quarry, and just the fact that we had enough room to run and do what we wanted to do.
You having over 600 acres to run around and get into things as a kid, that's heaven to me.
When I was growing up, I would have been all over that.
Oh, we were definitely all over it, and we were places we shouldn't have been for sure.
Oh, I'm sure you were. Yeah, I would have been too.
All right, Mark, please tell us about your encounter now. Give us every last detail that comes to mind.
Absolutely. This would have been in the late 80s, 87, 88, somewhere around there.

and during the summers one of the things that we would do is we would go down and on the river we

it ran into the main road which was a state road but you got to understand though especially where

there was probably i would say about five of those lights maybe
